Yeah, it's also like it was really, it was always kind of tense because like the direct brand sales team was selling to the same customers to like go try to sell to like Gap or whoever.
And then.

The partnership team is trying to sell to Gap's agency and at the end of the day it's all revenue but you just want to make sure there's like parity that like yeah it's not steeply discounted if they go through the agency or there's like some protections but yeah it was really like there was like always a war between like the sales team and the partnerships team because we're going after the same end clients at the end of the day.

Yeah one thing we're doing here differently they we finally hire different channel people who manage the partnerships and one thing that I advise them because we used to work I used to work at sprinkler with managing many of clients and being consultant as well and the same thing we always thought of like no this is my opportunity I need to get paid on this or like no I advise them to purchase you so you can just take this opportunity or whatnot.
Everyone was fighting and one thing that I work with the enablement team is that we can't do that because you're going to make enemies.
It's within the own company.
We're all trying to win together.
And so we're hopeful right now what they're setting up for rules of engagement is like no matter what it is, if we're working together, we all win together.
And it's helping everyone collaborate more efficiently.
Nice.

Yeah, the thing that takes the longest is the implementation part because I'm not sure if you've seen the AI platform or the SEO platform, but it requires a lot of research on what keywords you're currently using, running an audit, understanding the sitemaps, like what are they doing from the SEO standpoint, identifying the top keywords, and then implementing those keywords who become topics in LLMs, understanding what problems we need to monitor, and then building a whole map within the platform.
That is what takes a lot of the time.
The selling or the research and presenting part of a strategy session, I would call it, is pretty straightforward because we're having kind of a discussion.
Discussion of the business when implementing comes that is that takes a little bit longer.
So like how would it work?

I'm just curious how.
So an example, let's say you were an agency trying to work with us and or trying to you're using like a pro or guru account.
I actually have access to your instance.
So I'll just get the admin email, put it into the system from the back end.
I'll see what you're using, how often you're using it, basically all your information on the current subscription.
And then what I usually do is either share that information with.
The sales development team and let them know this would be a perfect fit for enterprise or this would be a perfect fit for dados or social listening or whatnot.
And then they go and run with that, send a few emails to more accounts than what I can do.

And then from there, we go and meet with that team and tell them, here's what we saw, share my screen, share what they're currently doing, and then go and share what they could potentially have if they want to upgrade and use these other features based on the data I found on them.
So usually these conversations are very data-driven, also very technical in what they're trying to achieve.

And also every conversation is different because if I'm dealing with a B2C versus B2B, the conversations end up being extremely different because their priorities, challenges, and goals are somewhat different.
And those are the conversations that I have.
That is usually how it looks.

They do the research and the information, and then they deal with that.

Yeah, so then what's that look like?
Like, okay, say they're on board, they want to upgrade.
You said implementation takes a long time.
What is the steps?
From implementation?
So once I know they are able to provide, since they're already clients of SEMrush, I have access to many of their keyword lists.
I export them via CSV.
Then what I do within the enterprise solution, I go and just migrate that data into there and start doing tags, start running some audits, and then after that, it's just very easily monitored.
What is very difficult is whenever they don't have their complete list inside SEMrush, and I have to ask them to send me that information.
So what usually happens is we meet, we have a strategy session, they go and do the commercial part or order forms.

Once that's done, I go and take much of their database, push it into the enterprise solution, start organizing, adding tags, structuring, organizing, upgrading all of their, pulling all of their content and making sure that I can run an audit on that.
And then after that, we just share that with them and I go over what the findings were.
And then from there, they usually are able to see everything, just go in and use everything to what they want to use.
Because there's like 30 different tools within the one, one single SEO tool.
Yeah.
And then within the AI platform, it's a little bit different.
What about if it's a, like, they just don't know what their strategy is.
They don't know what keywords, like, do you guys also help, Samrash also help strategy there too?
Or like, and is there like professional services or I'm not sure how it works there.
Like, I'm just curious, just because our clients, a lot of them.

Yeah, so my job is strategizing up around the business and where we fit in.
I am also able to provide, like, a quick 10 keyword that they can start implementing right away into their organization or within their website.
So we do that.
Let's say they want an extensive 1,500 keywords or 5,000 keywords.
We do.
That's where I am also going to be telling them, I can give you a set of, like, three keywords per page or what, you know, the highest conversion rate that you have in certain pages.
But if we do need to do an extensive whole site audit, then you will need to work with the professional services team.
And we do have professional services who will go and do that single job, which is keyword mapping, and they'll do it if they need to run it.
But I am able to run a simple audit and letting them know this is the data we found.
Here's what we recommend.
If you want something more extensively done by a human, professional services is there to help.
And is that ticketed?
Like you submit a ticket for professional services to help?
Yes, that is managed by another individual.
And he goes and provides those services.
But again, it's a lot of communication between different teams.
Yeah, but that's, yeah.

Yeah, we have, obviously SEMrush is way more of a SaaS tool and we're way more of an agency tool, but there's still a lot of overlap in what you're saying with just like, there is still the strategy element, there is still like handholding and sales throughout the whole thing.
So it's, there's a lot of overlap.

I would focus more on what content we need to create for social media.

And what we did with that is just using queries and researching what are some of the most trending topics.
And finding semantic related keywords or topics that we can input into the content piece.

Yeah.
Yep.

I use it primarily for research.

SEO, for example, like what are the things that we can recommend to them from the digital standpoint since they're so focused on other businesses?

basically like going back to school again.

mean, we're very...
